surface: Private Milestones 3–9 source/test/build and repository-stabilization implementation plus post-Milestone-9 public typed data-lifecycle and page-destroy gate-closure tranches: the native element/prop/event/ref slice; callback-demanded list cells; serializable native-resource handles; background platform hooks; a one-shot render-only main runtime; clone-safe first-tree snapshots, adoption, event handoff, mismatch repair, and cleanup; typed Engine __RenderPage/__UpdatePage/__UpdateGlobalProps registration with data-only page-data/global-props background delivery; typed native __DestroyLifetime registration with root-independent background logical teardown; Octane-owned main-thread/background-only function transforms, worklet events and refs, and bidirectional cancelable calls; retained Suspense/error/Activity behavior; dual-layer lazy chunks; acknowledged same-root portals; universal transition/deferred scheduling and conservative memo bailouts; compatible component wrappers and removed thread-site invalidation; a production Rspeedy application mode that specializes the same authored entry into main and background graphs and emits decoded entry and async .lynx.bundle artifacts; a facade over the official JavaScript testing environment; a complete 1,725-case pinned Vitest runner crosswalk; a permanent boolean-only list-defer lifecycle decision; and deterministic semantic preview/IFR source/build size guards. No Lynx Web, native bootstrap/first-paint, native identity, native data/destroy context or delivery/ordering/payload completeness, native worklet execution, native chunk execution, portal placement, allocation, scrolling, Native Module, custom-element, Android, iOS, or native performance claim is made.
divergences: The package adapts an audited @lynx-js/types@4.0.0 props/events slice into renderer-owned module declarations because the upstream events entry transitively loads global Element/JSX augmentation.
Background refs receive cloned acknowledgement-gated asynchronous handles, never live main-thread Element PAPI objects or synchronous layout.

List-item defer is permanently boolean-only; the ReactLynx defer.unmountRecycled object form is rejected. A recycled list item remains a logical Octane subtree while its physical native cell is absent. Refs detach and reattach through the optional universal host-attachment capability; viewport recycling alone does not run component or effect cleanup, so state and effects survive until logical removal.
Background event handlers receive detached data-only payloads without live event methods or native instances.

Rspeedy application mode compiles the same authored entry twice: the main layer resolves the package root to the synchronous first-screen facade and uses a PrimJS-safe one-shot renderer, while the background layer owns effects, refs, state, listeners, scheduling, and later updates.

Octane owns the 'main thread' and 'background only' directive transform, stable function identities, capture isolation, registries, activation tokens, and call lifecycle. It uses the pinned Lynx-compatible descriptor and worklet-event envelope at the host boundary without importing ReactLynx's component, Preact, transform, or worklet runtime.

Main-thread refs are explicit worklet-runtime cells updated from adopted Element PAPI nodes. A mounted hook owner preserves initialized state across calls, host detach still writes null, and the cell is collected after its host, owner, and activation lifetimes end. They are distinct from acknowledgement-gated background LynxNodesRef handles and do not make native measurement synchronous.

Platform APIs are background-only; the main-thread compiler preset rejects @octanejs/lynx/platform imports and statically visible NativeModules access. createLynxRoot() separately verifies the public background-only lynx.getJSModule() surface at runtime. installLynxMainThread() receives typed Engine lifecycle events and forwards data-only page-data/global-props messages; reloadTemplate is rejected rather than treated as an ordinary data update. The reload export only requests lynx.reload(); it is not a reconstructing framework reload receiver.

Native list hosts, list materializations, and background-root-scoped native-resource props are excluded from first-tree capture and have no Milestone 6 adoption claim. Nested lists, native Lynx proof for retained boundaries and lazy chunks, gestures, animations, and eager-main versus deferred-background boolean-defer semantics remain excluded; defer.unmountRecycled is an intentional divergence rather than a deferred feature.

Lynx portals are background-only and require a current, physically attached, acknowledgement-gated LynxPublicHandle from the same transported and universal root. First-screen, stale, detached, cross-root, text, and native-list targets reject before mutation; arbitrary native containers are not portal targets.
The synchronous first-screen lazy renderer is one-shot: it may commit an authored pending arm and prewarm independent chunks, but the retained background root owns later reveal or rejection.
Without AsyncContext, updates that occur while a universal async transition Action is pending share the same process-global entangled transition window; discrete event updates remain urgent.
ssr: Unsupported. Lynx first-screen adoption is a native runtime protocol, not HTML SSR or Octane hydration.
notes: Milestone 0 remains blocked on the native string-event receiver and reconstructing-reload contract, Explorer/Android/iOS verification of context, delivery, ordering, and payload completeness for the typed data and page-destroy paths, native bootstrap/first-paint, Web execution, and the Android/iOS engine matrix; this private implementation is not a technical preview.

Native __AddEvent token registration is public, but the production background callback receiver remains the private lynxCoreInject.tt.publishEvent hook; dispatchNativeEvent* is a source/test bridge only.

Milestone 5 connects CSS imports, CSS Modules, stylesheet/template sections, assets, source/debug output, runtime wrapping, and native encoding through the pinned framework-neutral Rspeedy packages. This is build evidence, not native style, asset-loading, or source-map execution evidence.

Octane submits a complete next dataset bag through __SetDataset. @lynx-js/testing-environment@0.3.0 implements that operation with Object.assign, so key deletion is adapter-call evidence only until a real-engine lane proves it.

The public types expose createSelectorQuery().select(string), while selectUniqueID is commented out. Octane therefore uses generation-scoped [octane-ref] selectors; the testing environment proves selection/setNativeProps only, so invoke/fields/path/measure, selector support, and layout results require real-engine evidence.

The official JavaScript environment models a 1,000-item logical list, callback-demanded materialization, native cell identity reuse, reorder, attachment ordering, and inert callbacks after teardown. It cannot establish Android/iOS native allocation, scrolling, layout, steady-state memory, or lifecycle behavior.

The deterministic Node-only allocation gate records 12 physical fake-PAPI cells for a 12-row visible window over 1,000 logical items, 988 reuses, zero remaining cells after teardown, and a 0.012 eager-allocation ratio. It is not a native memory or timing measurement.

Against exact Lynx SDK 3.9.0 source commit d7f13487, TemplateAssembler first dispatches __RenderPage, __UpdatePage, and __UpdateGlobalProps through the Engine ContextProxy when the named listener exists and only then falls back to the legacy global call. installLynxMainThread() now installs that public getEngine() candidate: __RenderPage sends page-data replace to seed current init data; ordinary __UpdatePage sends update for a merge, resetPageData === true sends reset so omitted keys are removed, and reloadTemplate === true is diagnosed/rejected without forwarding; __UpdateGlobalProps sends a global-props patch. The background store retains public __presetData fallback and publishes complete current snapshots. Source/unit tests cover seeding, merge, reset, global-props updates, replacement, subscription timing, and cleanup.

The typed data and page-destroy paths are source-integrated candidates only. Explorer, Android, and iOS must still prove native context, delivery, ordering, payload completeness, bootstrap/first-paint, and reload/destroy interaction. Global listener replacement, Native Module typing, public reload requests, and error reporting also have source/unit evidence only. Public native string-event delivery and reconstructing framework reload remain blocked contracts.
The app-owned Android/iOS Native Module and custom-element files under examples/native-capabilities are illustrative, excluded from package files, and not compiled or device-verified.

Boolean list-item defer metadata is accepted and forwarded, but all initial Octane list cells are callback-demanded regardless of that prop; this is not full ReactLynx defer behavior. The defer={{ unmountRecycled: true }} object form is rejected as a permanent lifecycle divergence: physical recycling detaches refs but preserves logical state and effects until item removal.
Nested <list> hosts are rejected during batch preparation in the initial contract rather than entering a partially supported recycling path.

Milestone 6 source and JavaScript-host tests cover synchronous one-shot rendering, an explicit entry-initialization synchronization gate, a clone-safe snapshot, compatible adoption without host allocation or restructuring, FIFO ordinary-event replay after background listener ownership, typed mismatch repair, and cleanup. This is not native first-frame, identity, or performance evidence.

Milestone 7 compiler, protocol, driver, and JavaScript-host tests cover thread directives and layer-specific import removal; clone-safe capture diagnostics; main-thread event/ref activation and release; calls queued before adoption; async values/errors; cancellation; and stale-root plus active/retained definition-revision rejection. Milestone 8 disposal additionally invalidates previously resolved never-activated descriptors and unregisters removed module sites without requiring a component export. The tests do not prove native main-thread execution, adopted-node identity, gesture/continuous-event latency, or device cleanup.

Milestone 8 source/build and JavaScript-host tests cover retained first-tree adoption, later Suspense/error/Activity behavior, abandoned attempts, pre-ACK rejection and accepted faults, same-root portal identity/order/retargeting, cached and parallel-warmed lazy components, a content-hashed dual-layer Rspeedy lazy bundle, transition pending/deferred behavior, conservative memo invalidation, compatible wrapper replacement, and removed thread-site invalidation. Native chunk loading, portal placement, transition timing, reload reconstruction, and resource/list migration remain unproven.

The pinned ReactLynx runner artifact classifies all 1,725 runnable JavaScript/TypeScript cases with zero unclassified entries; its 478 port, 185 differential, 39 intentional-divergence, 644 deferred, and 379 out-of-scope totals are disposition counts describing intended handling. No classification, including port or differential, proves that corresponding Octane behavior is implemented, that tests ran against Octane, or that parity passed. The source inventory separately classifies 89 source-defined Rust compiler cases in 11 files as out of scope.

The deterministic lynx-bundle-size gate builds the same semantic-checksummed app in background-preview and dual-thread IFR shapes, verifies thread ownership, records decoded/encoded raw, gzip, and Brotli bytes, requires exact background raw/gzip/Brotli metrics, and guards whole-artifact plus decoded-main gzip ratios. It is source/build size evidence only, not native timing, memory, first-paint, adoption, or device evidence.
Milestones 3–9 formal exits remain unmet until equivalent pinned ReactLynx fixtures and native capability samples are compared through public observations on Lynx Web, Explorer, Android, and iOS.

Omitting the Rspeedy plugin's thread option is the production application source/build path. The generated main graph installs the receiver, evaluates the same authored imports under the first-screen specialization, and releases its manual sync gate after synchronous initialization; the background graph evaluates those authored imports with the full renderer. Explicit background and main-thread options remain isolated compiler-graph diagnostics.
@octanejs/lynx/testing re-exports the pinned JavaScript testing environment's public classes and install/tree/event helpers. Its availability explicitly excludes native and device execution.

Required Lynx compatibility (minimum) and Lynx compatibility (current) CI lanes cover exact atomic source/build graphs. Both use Lynx SDK 3.9.0 (target 3.9), Rspeedy 0.16.0, Rsbuild 2.1.4, template plugin 0.13.0, CSS extract plugin 0.9.0, runtime wrapper 0.2.2, dev transport 0.3.0, runtime globals 0.0.7, tasm 0.0.39, testing environment 0.3.0, Lynx types 4.1.0, TypeScript 5.9.3, and the blocked Web control @lynx-js/web-core@0.22.2; minimum uses Rspack 2.1.3 and current uses Rspack 2.1.5 with a registry-drift check. Each packs strict external consumers and builds twice without changing the repository lockfile. The immutable audit/toolchain.json provenance covers the Phase 0/Milestone 5 subset, including the minimum Rspack edge and renderer-local Lynx types 4.0.0 declaration source; complete Milestone 9 lane versions come from the plugin source map, and the current lane's live registry check is not committed tarball-integrity provenance. This is not native-engine coverage.

Milestone 9's package/API review keeps @octanejs/lynx and @octanejs/rspeedy-plugin at private version 0.0.0 and leaves the universal renderer ABI experimental. Public stabilization still requires a framework-neutral native string-event receiver and reconstructing-reload contract; native verification of context, delivery, ordering, and payload completeness for the source-integrated typed data and page/background-destroy paths; a working Web transport; minimum/current toolchain execution on native engines plus Explorer/Android/iOS execution; native bootstrap/first-paint and identity adoption; worklet/ref/call, list lifecycle/allocation, lazy-chunk, portal, Native Module/custom-element, source-map, and reconstructing-reload proof; and comparable native semantic performance baselines. The private packages are not a technical preview.
